mission.title: Install and Configure K8up on Kubernetes
mission.description: K8up is a Kubernetes backup operator that simplifies the process of backing up Persistent Volume Claims (PVCs) and applications in a Kubernetes or OpenShift cluster. By using K8up, you can easily create schedules and manage credentials for backups, ensuring your data is safe and recoverable.

steps.title: Add the K8up Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the K8up Helm repository to your Helm client to access the K8up charts:
```bash
helm repo add k8up-io https://k8up-io.github.io/k8up
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install K8up CRDs
steps.description:
K8up CRDs must be installed separately before the Helm chart. Always upgrade CRDs before the chart:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/k8up-io/k8up/releases/download/k8up-4.8.6/k8up-crd.yaml --server-side
```

steps.title: Install K8up using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install K8up in your desired namespace (e.g., 'k8up-system'):
```bash
helm install k8up k8up-io/k8up --namespace k8up-system --create-namespace --version k8up-4.8.6
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the K8up pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace k8up-system
```

steps.title: Verify CRDs are registered
steps.description:
Confirm that all K8up Custom Resource Definitions are registered:
```bash
kubectl get crd | grep k8up.io
```
You should see backups, schedules, restores, prunes, checks, archives, snapshots, prebackuppods, and podconfigs.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of K8up will show the K8up pods running in the specified namespace, and you will be able to access the monitoring endpoint via port-forwarding. Additionally, resource limits should be configured as per your requirements.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add k8up-io https://k8up-io.github.io/k8up
helm repo update
helm install k8up k8up-io/k8up --namespace k8up-system --create-namespace --version k8up-4.8.6

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the K8up release, use the following command to uninstall it from the specified namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall k8up --namespace k8up-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
After uninstalling the Helm release, remove all K8up Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs):
```bash
kubectl get crd -o name | grep k8up.io | xargs kubectl delete
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace k8up-system
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, remove the K8up namespace and verify that it has been deleted:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace k8up-system
kubectl get namespaces | grep k8up-system
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up your current K8up configuration and resources. You can do this by exporting the current Helm release:
```bash
helm get values k8up --namespace k8up-system > k8up-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
First upgrade the CRDs, then update the Helm repository and upgrade the release:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/k8up-io/k8up/releases/latest/download/k8up-crd.yaml --server-side
helm repo update
helm upgrade k8up k8up-io/k8up --namespace k8up-system
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the K8up pods are running the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace k8up-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice that the K8up pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the pod to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace k8up-system
``` 
Look for any error messages that might indicate configuration issues or resource constraints.

troubleshooting.title: Backup jobs not running
troubleshooting.description:
If your backup jobs are not running as scheduled, check the status of the backup custom resources:
```bash
kubectl get backups --namespace k8up-system
``` 
Ensure that the schedules are correctly configured and that the K8up controller is running.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the K8up service, verify that the service is running and check the port-forwarding command:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace k8up-system
``` 
Ensure that the service is exposed correctly and that the port-forwarding command is still active.

troubleshooting.title: Persistent Volume Claims not bound
troubleshooting.description:
If the PVCs created by K8up are not bound, check the status of the PVCs:
```bash
kubectl get pvc --namespace k8up-system
``` 
Ensure that the storage class is available and that there are sufficient resources in your cluster.

prerequisites.description: You need a running Kubernetes cluster and the Helm and kubectl command-line tools installed.
